title: Texas Pastor Calls for Execution of Gay People, Then His Own Church Implodes
teaser: Pastor Dillon Awes of Stedfast Baptist Church delivered a sermon calling for gay people to be shot in the back of the head. Shortly after, his church's lead pastor was exposed for soliciting prostitutes and gambling, forcing the congregation to fracture.
body:
There is a certain theological irony in a pastor demanding capital punishment for the sins of others while his own house of worship is quietly accumulating the raw materials for a very different kind of scandal. That irony arrived in full for Stedfast Baptist Church in Watauga, Texas, where Pastor Dillon Awes delivered a sermon in June 2022 calling for gay people to be charged with crimes, convicted, sentenced to death, and shot in the back of the head. The sermon was not a momentary lapse. It was, by documented accounts, part of a recurring pattern.

According to NBC News reporting, Awes made these statements in a June 2022 sermon and had repeated similar calls in documented sermons across multiple years. The consistency of the rhetoric suggests this was not a rhetorical slip but a considered theological position. Awes presented execution as the appropriate governmental and spiritual response to homosexuality.

Stedfast Baptist Church has been designated an anti-LGBT hate group by the Southern Poverty Law Center. That designation predates the June 2022 sermon and reflects a broader institutional posture, not merely the opinions of one preacher on one Sunday. The church has operated within a network of affiliated Independent Fundamental Baptist congregations that have drawn similar scrutiny.

Awes framed his call for execution in explicitly governmental terms, arguing that the state should carry out the sentence. This is a meaningful rhetorical distinction: it is not merely an expression of personal moral disapproval but a direct policy prescription for lethal state violence. The sermon specified the method, the legal process, and the desired outcome with a degree of procedural detail that is, to put it charitably, unusual for a Sunday morning address.

The reaction to the sermon's wider circulation was predictable. Civil rights organizations and LGBTQ advocacy groups condemned the remarks publicly. News coverage amplified the sermon beyond the congregation's walls, which is presumably not the outcome Awes intended when he delivered what he considered a straightforward reading of scripture. Public scrutiny of fringe religious rhetoric has a way of making the fringe feel considerably less comfortable.

What followed at Stedfast Baptist Church added a layer of institutional accountability that no outside critic could have engineered more efficiently. The church's lead pastor was subsequently revealed to have solicited prostitutes and engaged in gambling. These are not offenses that sit comfortably alongside a ministry defined by its aggressive condemnation of other people's moral failures. The revelations forced a church split, fracturing the congregation that had enthusiastically hosted Awes's calls for execution.

The timing and nature of the lead pastor's conduct deserve some analytical attention. A congregation organized around the premise that certain categories of sinners deserve death by firearm was led, in part, by a man engaged in conduct that most traditional Christian denominations would classify as serious moral failure. This is not a peripheral irony. It is a structural one, embedded in the leadership itself.

Church splits are not uncommon in independent Baptist circles, where congregational autonomy means there is no denominational hierarchy to impose discipline or manage crises. When the crisis is the pastor, the available remedies are limited to departure, confrontation, or fragmentation. In this case, fragmentation appears to have been the outcome. The institution Awes helped define through his sermons did not survive its own leadership intact.

It is worth noting that Awes's documented pattern of repeating these calls across multiple years means this was not an aberration that the church leadership failed to catch in time. The rhetoric was known, recurring, and presumably endorsed or at minimum tolerated by those in authority over him. The subsequent exposure of that authority's conduct recontextualizes the entire enterprise in ways that require no additional editorial commentary.

Accountability journalism occasionally produces a story in which the subject provides all the necessary irony without any assistance from the reporter. This is one of those stories. A pastor who delivered serial sermons demanding the execution of a class of people he deemed irredeemably sinful was embedded in an institution whose own leadership was quietly demonstrating that moral condemnation is considerably easier to preach than to practice.

Awes has not, based on available reporting, walked back the substance of his execution sermons. The church split does not appear to have prompted any public theological reconsideration on his part. Whether the congregation that remained with him after the fracture has reflected on the relationship between their pastor's rhetoric and their institution's collapse is not documented. It would, however, be a productive reflection.

The Southern Poverty Law Center's hate group designation now sits alongside a public record of documented execution sermons, a church split precipitated by the lead pastor's conduct, and national news coverage that ensured none of this would remain a local story. For a ministry that presented itself as a repository of moral clarity, the accumulated record suggests something considerably more complicated. The gap between the standard Awes applied to others and the standard his institution met by its own conduct is not a small one. It is, by any reasonable measure, the size of a church.
